stories.story: Movies like 'All Is Lost' starring Robert Redford and Ang Lee's 'Life of Pi' depict individuals stranded at sea, but real-life survival for days, weeks, or months is incredibly rare. These aren't short-term incidents where people are quickly rescued by the U.S. Coast Guard. True survival relies on ingenuity, resourcefulness, and luck, not technology or nearby help, especially when food and water run out. Many such stories end sadly, with a sailor dying at sea, far more often than in rescues. Happy endings, though rare, do occur, often based on sole survivor accounts which are hard to prove or refute.
key_facts: Movies depict stranded at sea (All Is Lost, Life of Pi)
Robert Redford's role in All Is Lost
Ang Lee's Life of Pi
Real-life long-term survival at sea is hard to swallow
Miracles happen outside Hollywood
Not short-term rescues by U.S. Coast Guard
Rarer cases involve running out of food and water
Survival relies on ingenuity, resourcefulness, and luck
Many stories end sadly with death at sea
Happy endings occur, reported by rescue agencies
Sole survivor accounts are a big qualifier
explicit_unknowns: how many of these types of stories end sadly
no one else can prove or refute their accounts

stories.story: Louis Jordan, a sailor, set off on his 35-foot sailboat from South Carolina in late January, heading into the Gulf Stream to fish. His boat capsized, breaking its mast and his shoulder, turning his life upside down. Louis Jordan rationed water, collected fresh water in a bucket, and used laundry to trap fish for food. He also rigged a makeshift mast and sail, though his progress was slow, and his sailboat capsized two more times. Louis Jordan was eventually spotted by the crew of the German-flagged container ship, Houston Express, about 200 miles off the North Carolina coast on a Thursday. His father greeted Louis Jordan with a hug, admitting, 'I thought I lost you.'

stories.story: Jose Salvador Alvarenga began his journey from Paredon Viejo, Mexico's Pacific coast, in late 2012, though the exact date is debated. What was meant to be a one-day fishing trip turned into an arduous odyssey across the Pacific Ocean after winds blew his boat off course and a storm hit, causing the boat to lose its engine, radio, and GPS. Four weeks in, Jose Salvador Alvarenga's 23-year-old fishing companion, Ezequiel Cordova, died after refusing to eat raw birds. Jose Salvador Alvarenga survived by drinking rainwater, his own urine, and eating sea turtles for 13 or 14 months. He eventually arrived on Ebon Atoll in the Marshall Islands in January 2013, thousands of miles from where he started, and credited his faith in God for his survival.
key_facts: Jose Salvador Alvarenga's journey began in Paredon Viejo, Mexico's Pacific coast, late 2012
He disappeared after leaving until January 2013
He interacted with humans again on a remote atoll in the Marshall Islands

Ebon Atoll is about a 22-hour boat ride from Majuro, Marshall Islands capital

The El Salvador native's faith in God helped him survive
He thought, 'I am going to get out.'
explicit_unknowns: The exact date of his departure is up for debate (he says December, locals say November)

stories.story: Ron Ingraham, a tough 67-year-old fisherman from Hawaii, set off solo from Molokai around Thanksgiving. After bad weather hit, a huge wave struck his 25-foot sailboat, pushing its mast and Ron Ingraham into the water, though he used a rope to pull himself back in. A distress call initiated a 12,000 square mile search, which was called off on December 1, but his son, Zakary Ingraham, insisted, 'I don't feel like he's dead.' Twelve days after the distress call, Ron Ingraham was found alive, weak, hungry, and dehydrated, about 64 miles south of Honolulu, a testament to his resilience.
key_facts: Ron Ingraham is a fisherman in his late 60s from Hawaii
His son, Zakary Ingraham, compares him to Rambo
Ron Ingraham set off solo from Molokai, Hawaii, around Thanksgiving

Twelve days after the distress call, Ron Ingraham was picked up
He was found about 64 miles (103 kilometers) south of Honolulu
He was 'weak, hungry and dehydrated' and alive
The veteran fisherman headed back to shore after getting assurances his damaged boat would come with him

stories.story: In February 2012, 18-year-old Adrian Vasquez joined two friends for an overnight fishing expedition from San Carlos, Panama, on a small boat. After catching plenty of fish, their boat's engine died, leaving the trio without tools or navigational experience. Adrian Vasquez survived by eating raw fish and drinking rainwater as his boat, the Fifty Cents, drifted into the Pacific, but his two companions died, their bodies later thrown into the sea due to decomposition. After 26 days and nearly 600 miles, fishermen spotted Adrian Vasquez's tiny vessel north of the Galapagos Islands. The Ecuadorian navy rescued the teenage survivor, Adrian Vasquez, who had lost 20 pounds and showed severe signs of dehydration and lack of nutrition, a return his mother, Nilsa de la Cruz, called 'a miracle.'

They set off from San Carlos, Panama, on a small boat

His mother said, 'For us, this is an opportunity to get closer as a family, to be more understanding and loving.'
explicit_unknowns: It's not clear exactly how his two companions died

stories.story: Hiromitsu Shinkawa, a 60-year-old man, was at home in Minamisoma on March 11, 2011, when a 9.0 magnitude earthquake and devastating 30-foot tsunami struck, destroying his home and sweeping away his wife. Hiromitsu Shinkawa survived by holding onto his roof, which drifted over nine miles out to sea amidst debris. More than two days later, Hiromitsu Shinkawa was spotted waving a self-made red flag by crew aboard a Japanese Maritime Self-Defense Force destroyer. After being rescued, the 60-year-old Hiromitsu Shinkawa burst into tears and told his rescuers, 'I thought today was the last day of my life,' a miracle given nearly 16,000 people died in the disaster.
